glossary.def: The discipline of taking a model from notebook to a reliable production asset: versioned pipelines, experiment tracking, a model registry, CI/CD, monitoring, and the governance that makes the whole system accountable. The model is a small part of the system; MLOps is everything around it.

A directed acyclic graph of typed, re-runnable stages from raw data to served prediction, where every edge is a content-addressed artifact and every node is pinned to a code commit and config. The shipped asset is the recipe that regenerates the model, not the weights file alone.

Logging every training run's parameters, metrics, data hash, code commit, and artifacts so past results can be reproduced and compared. It answers 'what did we try and what happened?' — the searchable lab notebook the literal notebook never was.

A state machine over model versions with gated stages (None → Staging → Production → Archived). It enforces that at most one version is live per deployment slot, records who approved each transition, and makes rollback a one-line operation.

A promotion rule in which a newly trained challenger replaces the live champion only if it beats it on the same frozen holdout metric by a margin larger than the metric's noise, and breaks no guardrail. The incumbent is the default; the challenger must earn its place.

Data drift is a change in the input distribution P(x); concept drift is a change in the input-to-label relationship P(y|x). Only concept drift necessarily degrades accuracy, but both warrant monitoring because labels often arrive too late to watch error directly.

An unsupervised drift metric comparing a baseline distribution to a recent window, bucketed: PSI = Σ (a_i − e_i)·ln(a_i/e_i). Rules of thumb: <0.1 stable, 0.1–0.25 investigate, >0.25 act. It is a symmetrized cousin of KL divergence and detects only marginal drift.

The governance framework — anchored by the US Federal Reserve/OCC's SR 11-7 (2011) — treating models as a source of risk from both error and misuse, and prescribing robust development & documentation, independent validation, and governance with sign-off and monitoring.

refs.cite: Sculley, D. et al. (2015). Hidden Technical Debt in Machine Learning Systems. NeurIPS 2015.
Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System & OCC (2011). SR 11-7: Guidance on Model Risk Management.
Breck, E., Cai, S., Nielsen, E., Salib, M. & Sculley, D. (2017). The ML Test Score: A Rubric for ML Production Readiness and Technical Debt Reduction. IEEE Big Data 2017.
Kreuzberger, D., Kuhl, N. & Hirschl, S. (2022). Machine Learning Operations (MLOps): Overview, Definition, and Architecture. arXiv:2205.02302.
National Institute of Standards and Technology (2023). AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 1.0). NIST AI 100-1.
European Union (2024). Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 — the Artificial Intelligence Act. Official Journal of the EU.
